saida_bruta:
When the boys climbed the slope, she noticed them pause to confer. "You inquire, Niel."

"You should, George. She visits your family frequently, whereas she hardly even recognizes me to talk to."

While they lingered in front of the three steps leading to the porch, Mrs. Forrester appeared at the entrance and bowed politely, clutching one of the pink roses.

"Good-day, boys. Heading out for an outing?"

George Adams advanced and formally removed his wide straw hat. "Good-day, Mrs. Forrester. May we please fish and splash around the marsh, and eat our meal in the woods?"

"Of course. You have beautiful weather. When did school finish up? Do you not miss it? I expect Niel does. Judge Pommeroy informs me he reads quite a lot."

The boys chuckled, making Niel feel rather self-conscious.

"Off you go, and make certain you do not leave the meadow gate unlocked. Mr. Forrester strongly dislikes when cows wander onto his blue grass."

The boys trotted silently around the mansion to the grove entrance, then dashed yelling down the green hillsides beneath the lofty branches. Mrs. Forrester observed them through the kitchen glass until they vanished past the bend of the ridge. She spoke to her Bohemian helper.

"Mary, while you are busy ovenside today, bake a tray of biscuits for those lads. I will carry them over when they are eating their food."

"You should, George. She visits your family frequently, whereas she hardly even recognizes me to talk to."

While they lingered in front of the three steps leading to the porch, Mrs. Forrester appeared at the entrance and bowed politely, clutching one of the pink roses.

"Good-day, boys. Heading out for an outing?"

George Adams advanced and formally removed his wide straw hat. "Good-day, Mrs. Forrester. May we please fish and splash around the marsh, and eat our meal in the woods?"

"Of course. You have beautiful weather. When did school finish up? Do you not miss it? I expect Niel does. Judge Pommeroy informs me he reads quite a lot."

The boys chuckled, making Niel feel rather self-conscious.

"Off you go, and make certain you do not leave the meadow gate unlocked. Mr. Forrester strongly dislikes when cows wander onto his blue grass."

The boys trotted silently around the mansion to the grove entrance, then dashed yelling down the green hillsides beneath the lofty branches. Mrs. Forrester observed them through the kitchen glass until they vanished past the bend of the ridge. She spoke to her Bohemian helper.

"Mary, while you are busy ovenside today, bake a tray of biscuits for those lads. I will carry them over when they are eating their food."

saida_bruta:
As the lads trudged up the hill, she saw them stop to deliberate. "You ask, Niel."

"No, George. She goes to your place all the time, but she barely even knows who I am."

As they hesitated before the trio of steps to the veranda, Mrs. Forrester came to the door and nodded graciously, holding a single pink blossom.

"Good afternoon, boys. Off on an adventure?"

George Adams stepped forward and politely took off his broad straw hat. "Good afternoon, Mrs. Forrester. Could we fish and play in the creek, and have our lunch in the grove?"

"Certainly. It is a lovely day. When did your classes end? Aren't you lonely without them? I suppose Niel is. Judge Pommeroy tells me he is quite the reader."

The others laughed, which made Niel feel somewhat embarrassed.

"Run along, and be sure to close the pasture gate behind you. Mr. Forrester hates it when cattle get into his blue grass."

The boys hurried quietly past the house to the orchard gate, then ran shouting down the grassy slope under the tall oaks. Mrs. Forrester watched them from the pantry window until they disappeared around the curve of the hill. She turned to her Bohemian maid.

"Mary, while you have the stove hot today, make some cookies for those children. I will take them down while they are having their picnic."

saida_bruta:
As the boys made their way up the slope, she observed them pause to debate. "You ask, Niel."

"No, George. She visits your house constantly, but she hardly even recognizes me."

As they wavered at the base of the veranda steps, Mrs. Forrester appeared at the door and welcomed them warmly, clutching a solitary rose flower.

"Good afternoon, boys. Heading out for some fun?"

George Adams advanced and respectfully removed his wide straw hat. "Good afternoon, Mrs. Forrester. May we fish and swim in the stream, and eat our lunch in the woods?"

"Of course. It is a beautiful day. When did school let out? Don't you miss your classes? I imagine Niel does. Judge Pommeroy mentions he is a devoted reader."

The others chuckled, which caused Niel to blush with self-consciousness.

"Off you go, and remember to shut the meadow gate. Mr. Forrester detests it when the cows get into his lawn."

The children trotted silently past the residence to the garden entrance, then raced yelling down the lush hillside beneath the giant trees. Mrs. Forrester observed them from the kitchen window until they vanished behind the bend of the ridge. She spoke to her European servant.

"Mary, while you have the oven going now, bake some treats for those boys. I shall carry them down when they are eating their lunch."

saida_bruta:
Watching the youngsters head up the incline, she saw them stop to argue. "Ask her, Niel."

"No, George. She is always at your home, but she scarcely knows who I am."

As they hesitated near the bottom of the porch steps, Mrs. Forrester came to the doorway and greeted them kindly, holding a single rose blossom.

"Hello, boys. Off on an adventure?"

George Adams stepped forward and politely lifted his broad straw hat. "Hello, Mrs. Forrester. Can we swim and fish in the creek, and have our lunch in the forest?"

"Certainly. It is a lovely day. When did school finish? Don't you long for your lessons? I expect Niel does. Judge Pommeroy says he is an avid reader."

The rest laughed, making Niel turn red with embarrassment.

"Run along, and be sure to close the pasture gate. Mr. Forrester hates it when the cattle wander onto his grass."

The boys trotted quietly past the house to the orchard gate, then ran shouting down the grassy slope under the great trees. Mrs. Forrester watched them from the kitchen window until they disappeared around the curve of the hill. She turned to her foreign maid.

"Mary, since you have the stove hot today, make some cookies for those children. I will take them down while they are having their picnic."

saida_bruta:
Observing the boys walk up the slope, she noticed them pause to argue. "You ask, Niel."

"No, George. She visits your house often, but she scarcely recognizes me."

As they lingered by the base of the veranda, Mrs. Forrester appeared at the entrance and welcomed them warmly, clutching a solitary crimson rose.

"Hello, boys. Off on a journey?"

George Adams advanced and genteelly nudged his wide straw hat. "Good afternoon, Mrs. Forrester. May we fish and swim in the stream, and eat our lunch in the woods?"

"Of course. The weather is beautiful. When did your classes end? Do you not long for school? Surely Niel does. Judge Pommeroy mentions he loves books."

The companions chuckled, causing Niel to flush with self-consciousness.

"Off you go, and remember to latch the meadow gate. Mr. Forrester dislikes it when the cows stray onto his lawn."

The children trod softly past the residence to the garden gate, then rushed yelling down the grassy bank under the giant trees. Mrs. Forrester observed them from the pantry window until they vanished behind the bend of the ridge. She faced her Swedish helper.

"Mary, because you have the oven going now, bake some treats for those children. I shall carry them down while they enjoy their outing."

saida_bruta:
Watching the youngsters ascend the hill, she saw them stop to bicker. "You inquire, Niel."

"No, George. She frequents your home, whereas she barely knows me."

As they hesitated near the veranda's edge, Mrs. Forrester emerged at the doorway and greeted them cordially, holding a single red rose.

"Hi, boys. Setting off somewhere?"

George Adams stepped forward and politely tipped his broad straw hat. "Good day, Mrs. Forrester. Might we swim and fish in the creek, and have our picnic in the forest?"

"Naturally. It is a lovely day. When did your school term finish? Don't you miss your lessons? Surely Niel does. Judge Pommeroy says he is quite scholarly."

The boys giggled, making Niel blush with embarrassment.

"Run along, and be certain to close the pasture gate. Mr. Forrester hates it when the cattle wander onto his grass."

The youths tiptoed quietly past the house to the orchard gate, then sprinted shouting down the grassy slope beneath the massive trees. Mrs. Forrester watched them from the kitchen window until they disappeared around the curve of the hill. She turned to her Swedish maid.

"Mary, since the stove is hot right now, bake some cookies for those boys. I will take them down while they are having their picnic."

saida_bruta:

As they hesitated close to the veranda, Mrs. Forrester emerged at the door and greeted them cordially, clutching a single red rose.

"Hi, boys. Going on an outing?"

George Adams advanced and majorly tipped his broad straw hat. "Good day, Mrs. Forrester. Might we bathe and angle in the creek, and consume our meal in the forest?"

"Certainly. It is a lovely day. When did your academic term close? Do you not long for your lessons? Surely Niel does. Judge Pommeroy claims he is highly academic."

The youths giggled, making Niel turn crimson with bashfulness.

"Run along, and remember to close the meadow gate. Mr. Forrester hates it when the cattle wander onto his turf."

The youngsters crept quietly past the dwelling to the grove gate, then dashed shouting down the verdant slope beneath the massive timber. Mrs. Forrester watched them from the cookhouse pane until they disappeared past the curve of the mountain. She spoke to her Swedish servant.

"Mary, since the stove is warm now, bake some rolls for those boys. I will take them down while they are eating their food."

"Of course. It's a beautiful day. When did your school hand out holidays? Don't you miss your lessons? Surely Niel does. Judge Pommeroy says he is very studious."

The children chuckled, causing Niel to flush with embarrassment.

"Run along, and be sure to shut the pasture gate. Mr. Forrester dislikes it when the cows stray onto his lawn."

The lads walked quietly past the residence to the orchard gate, then ran yelling down the grassy incline under the giant trees. Mrs. Forrester tracked them from the kitchen pane until they vanished around the bend of the hill. She called to her Swedish maid.

"Mary, while the oven is hot today, make some buns for those boys. I will take them down while they have their picnic."

saida_bruta:
Watching the boys ascend the hill, she saw them stop to bicker. "You ask her, Niel."

"No, George. She goes to your home, but she barely knows me."

As they hesitated by the steps, Mrs. Forrester surfaced at the doorway and greeted them kindly, holding a single red rose.

"Hello, boys. Off on an adventure?"

George Adams walked up and courteously tipped his broad straw hat. "Good day, Mrs. Forrester. Might we fish and swim in your creek, and have our dinner in the trees?"

"Certainly. It is a lovely day. When did your school grant vacation? Don't you long for your classes? Surely Niel does. Judge Pommeroy claims he is quite scholarly."

The youngsters laughed, making Niel turn red with shame.

"Off you go, and remember to close the pasture gate. Mr. Forrester hates it when the cattle wander onto his grass."

The boys slipped quietly past the house to the orchard gate, then raced shouting down the grassy slope beneath the massive trees. Mrs. Forrester watched them from the kitchen window until they disappeared around the curve of the hill. She called out to her Swedish maid.

"Mary, while the stove is hot today, bake some rolls for those children. I will carry them down during their lunch."

Observing the youngsters climb the slope, she noticed them pause to argue. "You request it, Niel."

"No, George. She visits your house, but she hardly knows me."

As they lingered near the stairs, Mrs. Forrester appeared at the entrance and welcomed them warmly, clutching a solitary red blossom.

"Greetings, boys. Heading out on an excursion?"

George Adams stepped forward and politely tipped his wide straw hat. "Good afternoon, Mrs. Forrester. May we fish and bathe in your stream, and eat our lunch among the trees?"

"Of course. It is a beautiful day. When did your school close for break? Don't you miss your lessons? Surely Niel must. Judge Pommeroy mentions he is very studious."

The children chuckled, causing Niel to flush with embarrassment.

"Run along, and be sure to shut the meadow gate. Mr. Forrester dislikes it when the cows stray onto his lawn."

The boys glided silently past the dwelling to the garden gate, then ran yelling down the leafy hillside under the giant trees. Mrs. Forrester observed them from her kitchen window until they vanished past the bend of the ridge. She shouted to her Swedish helper.

"Mary, while the oven is warm today, make some buns for those boys. I shall bring them down for their midday meal."
